title: Ayahuasca Triggered My Kundalini: What Happens When Plant Medicine Opens the Gate

body:
Ayahuasca Triggered My Kundalini: What Happens When Plant Medicine Opens the Gate

You're here, aren't you? Probably 3 AM, scrolling, heart pounding, convinced you're losing your damn mind. I know that feeling. I’ve lived it. You took plant medicine - maybe ayahuasca, maybe something else - hoping for healing, for insight, for a breakthrough. Instead, you got a breakdown. Or something that feels a lot like it. And now you’re wondering what the hell is happening to you. You’re not alone. Not even close. I'll tell you what, that's where I was too.

My story? Ayahuasca. A powerful, beautiful, terrifying teacher. I went in seeking answers, like so many do. I came out with something far more intense, something I didn't even know existed: a full-blown kundalini awakening. It wasn't gentle, it wasn't graceful, and it sure as hell wasn't what the retreat brochure promised. It was a chaotic, often agonizing, years-long process that ripped my life apart and then, slowly, painstakingly, put it back together in a way I never could've imagined. You're probably feeling that ripping right now, aren't you? That’s what it does. It's not a joke. It's not some spiritual fad you can just dabble in. This is real, and it’s hitting you hard. Right?

Here's the thing: plant medicines, especially potent ones like ayahuasca, are incredible catalysts. They can open doors you didn't even know were there. Sometimes, those doors lead to beautiful gardens. Other times, they lead to a raging inferno. And if you're reading this, you're likely standing in the middle of that inferno, wondering if you'll ever get out. I get it. I really do. You're scared, you're confused, and you probably feel completely isolated. Most people don't talk about this stuff, because most people don't understand it. They'll tell you it's a "bad trip" or a "psychotic break." And yeah, it can feel like both of those things. But it's often something more, something deeper, something spiritual. It's an activation. And it's a lot to handle.

The Shock of the System: When Your Body Becomes a Battleground

So, what does this actually feel like? Forget everything you thought you knew about spiritual experiences. This isn't all bliss and light. Not at first. For me, it started subtly, a low hum after the ceremony. Then it ramped up. Fast. It felt like electricity running through my spine, sometimes a gentle current, other times a violent surge that felt like I was being electrocuted from the inside out. My body was constantly vibrating. I couldn't sleep. My nervous system was completely fried. I'd have spontaneous muscle spasms, tremors, intense heat flashes, then sudden chills. My digestion went haywire. My heart would race, then slow to an alarming crawl. It was like my body was fighting itself, or purging something ancient and deep. You might be experiencing similar physical sensations. They're terrifying because doctors don't often have answers for them. They'll run tests, find nothing, and then look at you like you're making it up or, worse, that you're crazy. Know what I mean?

The energy itself is relentless. It feels like a separate entity, but it's you. It's your own life force, your kundalini, awakened and trying to move through blockages. And believe me, we all have blockages. Trauma, repressed emotions, limiting beliefs - it all gets in the way. When this energy hits those walls, it creates intense pressure, pain, and discomfort. I felt like I was being stretched, pulled apart, and put back together again, over and over. It's not a pleasant process. It's raw, it's visceral, and it demands your full attention. You can't ignore it. It won't let you. It's like having an internal furnace stoked to maximum, and you're just trying to survive the heat.

I remember days - weeks - where I couldn't function. Just lying on the floor, trying to breathe through the intensity. The world outside felt distant, unreal. My senses were heightened to an unbearable degree. Sounds were too loud, lights too bright. I felt exposed, vulnerable, like all my layers of protection had been stripped away. And in many ways, they had. That's what this process does. It strips you bare. It forces you to confront everything you've avoided, everything you've buried. It's not optional. Once it's activated, it's on. And you have to ride it out. There's no turning back. That's the truth of it.

The Mental and Emotional Vortex

Beyond the physical, there's the mental and emotional storm. Oh, the storm. My mind became a battlefield. Intrusive thoughts, existential dread, paranoia - you name it, I experienced it. It felt like my ego, my sense of self, was dissolving. And that's incredibly disorienting and frightening. Who are you when you don't know who you are anymore? The boundaries between myself and the world blurred. I questioned everything: my sanity, my reality, my purpose. I thought I was going crazy, truly. I’d have moments of intense clarity and connection, followed by crushing anxiety and despair. It's a rollercoaster of extremes, and you're strapped in for the ride whether you like it or not.

Old traumas, things I thought I'd dealt with or forgotten, came roaring back to the surface. It was like a projector playing my entire life's pain on an endless loop. And not just my pain. Sometimes it felt like collective pain, ancestral pain. It's overwhelming. You feel everything, all at once, with no filter. It's like having your emotional skin peeled off. You're raw. Exposed. And you're constantly asking, "When will this end?" I'll tell you, that question is a constant companion during this time. You're probably asking it right now. It's okay. That's normal. That's part of the process. You're not alone in that desperate plea for it to just stop. It's exhausting.

I remember one night, I was convinced I was dying. My body was convulsing, my mind racing, and I felt this immense pressure in my head, like my skull was going to crack open. I called a friend, crying, telling them goodbye. They didn't know what to say. No one does. This isn't something you can easily explain to someone who hasn't experienced it. That isolation is one of the hardest parts. You feel like an alien in your own life. You look normal on the outside - maybe a bit tired, a bit stressed - but inside, you're a hurricane. It's a secret battle you're fighting, and it's draining every ounce of your energy. Does that land with you? I bet it does.

Finding Your Way Through the Fire: Survival Strategies

So, what do you do when you're in the thick of it? When you feel like you're drowning? First, and this is crucial: don't panic. I know, easier said than done. But panic just fuels the fire. You need to learn to surrender, even when every fiber of your being is screaming in protest. This isn't something you can fight. It's something you have to flow with, however terrifying that sounds. It's a river, and you're in it. You can try to swim against the current, or you can learn to float. Floating is less exhausting. Trust me on that.

Here's what helped me, and what I've seen help countless others who've been through this particular hell:

  • Grounding, grounding, grounding: This is your lifeline. When you feel like you're floating away, losing touch with reality, you need to bring yourself back to your body, back to the earth. Walk barefoot on the grass. Eat nourishing, simple food. Take a warm bath. Focus on your breath - deep, slow breaths into your belly. Keep things routine and simple. Don't try to do too much. Your nervous system is already overloaded.
  • Movement: Gentle movement is key. Not intense exercise, which can sometimes stir the energy even more. Think walking, gentle yoga, stretching, tai chi. It helps the energy move through your body rather than getting stuck and creating pressure. It’s like a pressure valve.
  • Creative Expression: This energy needs an outlet. If you don't give it one, it'll create one, often in disruptive ways. Write, draw, paint, sing, dance - whatever allows you to express what's happening inside you. It doesn't have to be good; it just has to be.
  • Find Support (the right kind): This is hard, because not everyone understands. You need people who won't dismiss your experience, who won't tell you you're crazy. Look for spiritual teachers, therapists, or coaches who have experience with spiritual emergence or kundalini. They exist. They're rare, but they're out there. And if you can't find one, find someone who can just listen without judgment. Someone who can hold space for your pain and confusion. I'll tell you what, finding the right support is like finding an oasis in the desert. It's everything. I relied heavily on books like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) and When Spirit Leaps: Navigating the Process of Spiritual Awakening by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link). They made me feel less alone.
  • Radical Self-Care: This isn't a luxury; it's a necessity. Rest when you need to rest. Say no to things that drain your energy. Prioritize sleep, even if it's broken. Eat well. Hydrate. Treat your body like a temple, because right now, it's undergoing a deep transformation. You're literally being rewired. Give it the resources it needs.
  • Education: Learn everything you can about kundalini. Understanding what's happening can reduce the fear. Books like The Kundalini Experience by Lee Sannella (paid link) and The Kundalini Guide by Bonnie Greenwell (paid link) were invaluable to me. They gave me language for what I was experiencing, and that made a huge difference.

The Long Haul: Patience and Persistence

This isn't a quick fix. This isn't something that resolves in a few weeks or even a few months. Kundalini awakening, especially one triggered so intensely by plant medicine, is a process that can take years. Yes, years. I know that's probably not what you want to hear, but I'm not going to sugarcoat it. It took me a long time to even begin to integrate what had happened. And honestly, it's still an ongoing journey. There are waves, periods of intensity followed by periods of calm. You learn to ride them. You learn to anticipate them. You learn to trust that you will get through them.

You're not going back to who you were before. That person is gone. This process is a deep, irreversible transformation. And that's okay. It's more than okay. It's ultimately a gift, even though it feels like a curse right now. You're being remade. You're shedding old skins, old ways of being that no longer serve you. It's painful because it's growth. It's like a caterpillar dissolving into goo inside its chrysalis before it can become a butterfly. You're in the goo phase, my friend. It's messy, it's uncomfortable, but it's leading somewhere new. Somewhere more authentic, more aligned with your true self.

The impatience is brutal, I know. You want it to be over. You want your old life back. But you can't force it. You can only surrender to the timing of the universe, to the intelligence of your own system. It's doing what it needs to do. Your job is to support it, to create a safe container for the process. Be gentle with yourself. Be compassionate. You're going through something incredibly difficult, something most people will never experience. Give yourself credit for surviving. Give yourself credit for showing up every day, even when it feels impossible.

The Aftermath: A New Way of Being

After the initial storm passed - and it does pass, I promise you - things started to shift. Slowly. Gradually. I began to feel more integrated, more whole. The intense physical symptoms lessened. The mental chatter quieted. I found a new sense of peace, a deeper connection to myself and to something larger than myself. My intuition became incredibly strong. My priorities changed completely. What I once valued seemed trivial, and what I once dismissed became deeply important. I saw the world, and myself, with new eyes. It's like a complete system upgrade. You're not just a slightly better version of yourself; you're operating on a different frequency entirely.

I'm not going to lie; it wasn't easy. There were many dark nights of the soul, many moments where I questioned everything. But looking back, I wouldn't trade the experience for anything. It broke me open in a way nothing else could have. It forced me to face my deepest fears, my deepest wounds, and to heal them. It showed me my own strength, my own resilience. It taught me what true surrender means. It taught me that life is far stranger and more mysterious than we ever imagine. And that's a beautiful thing.

So yeah, if you're reading this at 3 AM, feeling completely lost and terrified, just know this: you're not crazy. You're not alone. And you're going to get through this. It's a journey, not a destination. And it's going to change you in ways you can't even fathom right now. Hang in there. Breathe. Ground yourself. Seek out the right support. And trust the process. You've got this, even if it doesn't feel like it. You're stronger than you know. Much, much stronger. Truth is, you're becoming who you were always meant to be. It's just a really intense way to get there. But here's the thing, you're not broken, you're breaking open. There's a huge difference. Hold onto that. It's going to be okay.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
image_alt: Ayahuasca Triggered My Kundalini: What Happens When Plant Medicine Opens the Gate
